title: Urticaria

presenting_history.text: Defines urticaria as a mast-cell-driven disease presenting with wheals, angioedema, or both and distinguishes acute and chronic forms.
presenting_history.rationale: Supports mapped urticaria, hives, wheals, angioedema, acute or chronic classification, and compatible observed skin or swelling fields.
presenting_history.evidence_extract: Defines urticaria as a mast-cell-driven disease presenting with wheals, angioedema, or both and distinguishes acute and chronic forms.

source.title: The international EAACI/GA²LEN/EuroGuiDerm/APAAACI guideline for the definition, classification, diagnosis, and management of urticaria

treatment_and_management.text: States that chronic spontaneous or inducible urticaria can impair quality of life and work or school performance and that the guideline covers classification, diagnosis, and management.
treatment_and_management.rationale: Supports mapped acute versus chronic context and quality-of-life impact only; detailed management text was not accessible.
treatment_and_management.evidence_extract: States that chronic spontaneous or inducible urticaria can impair quality of life and work or school performance and that the guideline covers classification, diagnosis, and management.

source.title: The international EAACI/GA²LEN/EuroGuiDerm/APAAACI guideline for the definition, classification, diagnosis, and management of urticaria

referral.text: Supports documenting known allergen exposure, food or drug triggers, action-plan discussion, and specialist follow-up after suspected anaphylaxis.
referral.rationale: Supports mapped food or medicine trigger, action-plan, return, and clinician-arranged allergy follow-up documentation after suspected anaphylaxis.
referral.evidence_extract: Supports documenting known allergen exposure, food or drug triggers, action-plan discussion, and specialist follow-up after suspected anaphylaxis.

source.title: Emergency treatment of anaphylaxis: Guidelines for healthcare providers

safety_netting.text: Recognises rapid symptoms with airway, breathing, or circulation problems and usually skin or mucosal changes such as urticaria or angioedema; skin changes alone are not anaphylaxis.
safety_netting.rationale: Supports mapped breathing, lip or tongue swelling, dizziness or syncope, widespread swelling, mucosal, and known-trigger warnings while explicitly rejecting skin-only anaphylaxis inference.
safety_netting.evidence_extract: Recognises rapid symptoms with airway, breathing, or circulation problems and usually skin or mucosal changes such as urticaria or angioedema; skin changes alone are not anaphylaxis.

source.title: Emergency treatment of anaphylaxis: Guidelines for healthcare providers

source_limitations: Itching, episodic duration, dermographism, fever, broad allergy labels, blood tests, generic medication plans, fixed one-to-two-week follow-up, and routine urticaria management remain unsupported by opened exact sections.
Skin or mucosal changes alone must not be labelled anaphylaxis; acute urticaria remains clinician-stated.
Qualified clinician review has not been completed.
All prior supported legacy-item mappings were removed because canonical item-level applicability and workflow-specific rationale were not present in explicit repository records.
curation_policy: Direct guideline curation: retain only explicitly mapped content; rewrite only with explicit item mapping; remove unmapped legacy content; add only committed exact-section evidence summaries.
